problem.title: Shiori Miyagi and Maximum Array Score

sources.notes: Maximize score over a strictly increasing chosen array with divisibility-based value contribution.
Official editorial defines dp over a_i-i, then removes one dimension with a Fenwick tree supporting prefix maximum queries and point max updates.

annotation.summary: Choose a strictly increasing array within bounds to maximize a divisibility-based score.
annotation.constraints: The naive two-dimensional DP is too large; only positions with nonzero contribution should be updated.
annotation.core_idea: Let j = a_i - i, which must be nondecreasing for a strictly increasing array. The DP transition asks for the best previous state with offset at most j plus the value contribution v(i,i+j). This is a prefix maximum transition, so a Fenwick tree or segment tree stores max dp values while iterating i and processing useful j values in reverse.
annotation.complexity: O((m-n) log n log(m-n) + n) per test case.
tricks: transform strict increase into nondecreasing offsets

solution_variants.name: fenwick-prefix-max-dp
solution_variants.summary: Optimize dp[i][j]=max_{k<=j}dp[i-1][k]+v(i,i+j) with a Fenwick tree over offsets.

tags.evidence: The central recurrence is a prefix maximum over previous DP states.
tags.description: Dynamic programming optimized by prefix maximum/minimum aggregates over previous states.
tags.created_reason: This differs from prefix-sum DP and is useful for range maximum transition problems.

tags.evidence: The official solution uses a Fenwick tree to support prefix max queries and point max updates.
tags.description: Fenwick tree / Binary Indexed Tree for prefix aggregate queries and point updates.
tags.created_reason: The existing data-structure tag is too broad for searching Fenwick-specific problems.

tags.evidence: The score contribution v(i,x) counts divisibility exponent-like behavior.
tags.description: Reasoning based on divisibility, multiples, or exponent of divisibility.
tags.created_reason: Divisibility appears often without requiring heavier number-theory machinery.
